{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_bdrk_01H64eGozxYjuoFiiFCE445k","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→// slang-emit-spirv.cpp\n     2→\n     3→#include \"../core/slang-memory-arena.h\"\n     4→#include \"slang-compiler.h\"\n     5→#include \"slang-emit-base.h\"\n     6→#include \"slang-ir-call-graph.h\"\n     7→#include \"slang-ir-entry-point-decorations.h\"\n     8→#include \"slang-ir-insts.h\"\n     9→#include \"slang-ir-layout.h\"\n    10→#include \"slang-ir-redundancy-removal.h\"\n    11→#include \"slang-ir-spirv-legalize.h\"\n    12→#include \"slang-ir-spirv-snippet.h\"\n    13→#include \"slang-ir-util.h\"\n    14→#include \"slang-ir.h\"\n    15→#include \"slang-lookup-spirv.h\"\n    16→#include \"spirv/unified1/spirv.h\"\n    17→\n    18→#include <type_traits>\n    19→\n    20→namespace Slang\n    21→{\n    22→\n    23→// Our goal in this file is to convert a module in the Slang IR over to an\n    24→// equivalent module in the SPIR-V intermediate language.\n    25→//\n    26→// The Slang IR is (intentionally) similar to SPIR-V in many ways, and both\n    27→// can represent shaders at similar levels of abstraction, so much of the\n    28→// translation involves one-to-one translation of Slang IR instructions\n    29→// to their SPIR-V equivalents.\n    30→//\n    31→// SPIR-V differs from Slang IR in some key ways, and the SPIR-V\n    32→// specification places many restrictions on how the IR can be encoded.\n    33→// In some cases we will rely on earlier IR passes to convert Slang IR\n    34→// into a form closer to what SPIR-V expects (e.g., by moving all\n    35→// varying entry point parameters to global scope), but other differences\n    36→// will be handled during the translation process.\n    37→//\n    38→// The logic in this file relies on the formal [SPIR-V Specification].\n    39→// When we are making use of or enforcing some property from the spec,\n    40→// we will try to refer to the relevant section in comments.\n    41→//\n    42→// [SPIR-V Specification]: https://www.khronos.org/registry/spir-v/specs/unified1/SPIRV.html\n    43→\n    44→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    45→//\n    46→// > A SPIR-V module is a single linear stream of words.\n    47→\n    48→\n    49→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    50→//\n    51→// > All remaining words are a linear sequence of instructions.\n    52→// > Each instruction is a stream of words\n    53→//\n    54→// After a fixed-size header, the contents of a SPIR-V module\n    55→// is just a flat sequence of instructions, each of which is\n    56→// just a sequence of words.\n    57→//\n    58→// In principle we could try to emit instructions directly\n    59→// in one pass as a stream of words, but there are additional\n    60→// constraints placed by the SPIR-V encoding that would make\n    61→// a single-pass strategy very hard, so we don't attempt it.\n    62→//\n    63→// [2.4 Logical Layout of a Module]\n    64→//\n    65→// SPIR-V imposes some global ordering constraints on instructions,\n    66→// such that certain instructions must come before or after others.\n    67→// For example, all `OpCapability` instructions must come before any\n    68→// `OpEntryPoint` instructions.\n    69→//\n    70→// While the SPIR-V spec doesn't use such a term, we will take\n    71→// the enumeration of the ordering in Section 2.4 and use it to\n    72→// define a list of *logical sections* that make up a SPIR-V module.\n    73→\n    74→/// Logical sections of a SPIR-V module.\n    75→enum class SpvLogicalSectionID\n    76→{\n    77→    Capabilities,\n    78→    Extensions,\n    79→    ExtIntInstImports,\n    80→    MemoryModel,\n    81→    EntryPoints,\n    82→    ExecutionModes,\n    83→    DebugStringsAndSource,\n    84→    DebugNames,\n    85→    Annotations,\n    86→    ConstantsAndTypes,\n    87→    GlobalVariables,\n    88→    FunctionDeclarations,\n    89→    FunctionDefinitions,\n    90→\n    91→    Count,\n    92→};\n    93→\n    94→// The registered id for the Slang compiler.\n    95→static const uint32_t kSPIRVSlangCompilerId = 40 << 16;\n    96→\n    97→// While the SPIR-V module is nominally (according to the spec) just\n    98→// a flat sequence of instructions, in practice some of the instructions\n    99→// are logically in a parent/child relationship.\n   100→//\n   101→// In particular, functions \"own\" the instructions between an `OpFunction`\n   102→// and the matching `OpFunctionEnd`. We can also think of basic\n   103→// blocks within a function as owning the instructions between\n   104→// an `OpLabel` (which represents the bloc) and the next label\n   105→// or the end of the function.\n   106→//\n   107→// Furthermore, the common case is SPIR-V is that an instruction\n   108→// that defines some value must appear before any instruction\n   109→// that uses that value as an operand. This property is often true\n   110→// in a Slang IR module, but isn't strictly enforced for things at\n   111→// the global scope.\n   112→//\n   113→// To deal with the above issues, our strategy will be to emit\n   114→// SPIR-V instructions into a lightweight intermediate structure\n   115→// that simplifies dealing with ordering constraiints on\n   116→// instructions.\n   117→//\n   118→// We will start by forward-declaring the type we will\n   119→// use to represent instructions:\n   120→//\n   121→struct SpvInst;\n   122→\n   123→// Next, we will define a base type that can serve as a parent\n   124→// to SPIR-V instructions. Both the logical sections defined\n   125→// earlier and instructions such as functions will be used\n   126→// as parents.\n   127→\n   128→/// Base type for SPIR-V instructions and logical sections of a module\n   129→///\n   130→/// Holds and supports appending to a list of child instructions.\n   131→struct SpvInstParent\n   132→{\n   133→public:\n   134→    /// Add an instruction to the end of the list of children\n   135→    void addInst(SpvInst* inst);\n   136→\n   137→    /// Dump all children, recursively, to a flattened list of SPIR-V words\n   138→    void dumpTo(List<SpvWord>& ioWords);\n   139→\n   140→    /// The first child, if any.\n   141→    SpvInst* m_firstChild = nullptr;\n   142→\n   143→    /// A pointer to the null pointer at the end of the linked list.\n   144→    ///\n   145→    /// If the list of children is empty this points to `m_firstChild`,\n   146→    /// while if it is non-empty it points to the `nextSibling` field\n   147→    /// of the last instruction.\n   148→    ///\n   149→    SpvInst* m_lastChild = nullptr;\n   150→};\n   151→\n   152→// A SPIR-V instruction is then (in the general case) a potential\n   153→// parent to other instructions.\n   154→\n   155→/// A type to represent a SPIR-V instruction to be emitted.\n   156→///\n   157→/// This type alows the instruction to be built up across\n   158→/// multiple steps in a mutable fashion.\n   159→///\n   160→struct SpvInst : SpvInstParent\n   161→{\n   162→    // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   163→    //\n   164→    // > Each instruction is a stream of words\n   165→    //\n   166→    // > Opcode: The 16 high-order bits are the WordCount of the instruction.\n   167→    // >         The 16 low-order bits are the opcode enumerant.\n   168→    //\n   169→    // We will store the \"opcode enumerant\" directly in our\n   170→    // intermediate structure, and compute the word count on\n   171→    // the fly when writing an instruction to an output buffer.\n   172→\n   173→    /// The SPIR-V opcode for the instruction\n   174→    SpvOp opcode;\n   175→\n   176→    // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   177→    //\n   178→    // > Optional instruction type <id> (presence determined by opcode)\n   179→    // > Optional instruction Result <id> (presence determined by opcode)\n   180→    // > Operand 1 (if needed)\n   181→    // > Operand 2 (if needed)\n   182→    // > ...\n   183→    //\n   184→    // We represent the remaining words of the instruction (after\n   185→    // the opcode word) as an undifferentiated array. Any code\n   186→    // that encodes an instruction is responsible for knowing the\n   187→    // opcode-specific data that is required.\n   188→    //\n   189→    // Our code does not need to process instruction operands after\n   190→    // they have been written into a `SpvInst`. If we ever had\n   191→    // cases where we needed to do post-processing, then we would\n   192→    // need to store a more refined representation here.\n   193→\n   194→    /// The additional words of the instruction after the opcode\n   195→    SpvWord* operandWords = nullptr;\n   196→    /// The amount of operand words\n   197→    uint32_t operandWordsCount = 0;\n   198→\n   199→    // We will store the instructions in a given `SpvInstParent`\n   200→    // using an intrusive linked list.\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. IR over to an\n    24→// equivalent module in the SPIR-V intermediate language.\n    25→//\n    26→// The Slang IR is (intentionally) similar to SPIR-V in many ways, and both\n    27→// can represent shaders at similar levels of abstraction, so much of the\n    28→// translation involves one-to-one translation of Slang IR instructions\n    29→// to their SPIR-V equivalents.\n    30→//\n    31→// SPIR-V differs from Slang IR in some key ways, and the SPIR-V\n    32→// specification places many restrictions on how the IR can be encoded.\n    33→// In some cases we will rely on earlier IR passes to convert Slang IR\n    34→// into a form closer to what SPIR-V expects (e.g., by moving all\n    35→// varying entry point parameters to global scope), but other differences\n    36→// will be handled during the translation process.\n    37→//\n    38→// The logic in this file relies on the formal [SPIR-V Specification].\n    39→// When we are making use of or enforcing some property from the spec,\n    40→// we will try to refer to the relevant section in comments.\n    41→//\n    42→// [SPIR-V Specification]: https://www.khronos.org/registry/spir-v/specs/unified1/SPIRV.html\n    43→\n    44→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    45→//\n    46→// > A SPIR-V module is a single linear stream of words.\n    47→\n    48→\n    49→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    50→//\n    51→// > All remaining words are a linear sequence of instructions.\n    52→// > Each instruction is a stream of words\n    53→//\n    54→// After a fixed-size header, the contents of a SPIR-V module\n    55→// is just a flat sequence of instructions, each of which is\n    56→// just a sequence of words.\n    57→//\n    58→// In principle we could try to emit instructions directly\n    59→// in one pass as a stream of words, but there are additional\n    60→// constraints placed by the SPIR-V encoding that would make\n    61→// a single-pass strategy very hard, so we don't attempt it.\n    62→//\n    63→// [2.4 Logical Layout of a Module]\n    64→//\n    65→// SPIR-V imposes some global ordering constraints on instructions,\n    66→// such that certain instructions must come before or after others.\n    67→// For example, all `OpCapability` instructions must come before any\n    68→// `OpEntryPoint` instructions.\n    69→//\n    70→// While the SPIR-V spec doesn't use such a term, we will take\n    71→// the enumeration of the ordering in Section 2.4 and use it to\n    72→// define a list of *logical sections* that make up a SPIR-V module.\n    73→\n    74→/// Logical sections of a SPIR-V module.\n    75→enum class SpvLogicalSectionID\n    76→{\n    77→    Capabilities,\n    78→    Extensions,\n    79→    ExtIntInstImports,\n    80→    MemoryModel,\n    81→    EntryPoints,\n    82→    ExecutionModes,\n    83→    DebugStringsAndSource,\n    84→    DebugNames,\n    85→    Annotations,\n    86→    ConstantsAndTypes,\n    87→    GlobalVariables,\n    88→    FunctionDeclarations,\n    89→    FunctionDefinitions,\n    90→\n    91→    Count,\n    92→};\n    93→\n    94→// The registered id for the instructions, in practice some of the instructions\n    99→// are logically in a parent/child relationship.\n   100→//\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it Core Implementation: `emitDebugVarDeclaration` (Lines 3797-3847)\n```cpp\nSpvInst* emitDebugVarDeclaration(SpvInstParent* parent, IRDebugVar* debugVar)\n{\n    // For every DebugVar, we will declare:\n    // - An OpDebugLocalVariable `spvDebugLocalVar`\n    // - An OpVariable `actualHelperVar`\n    // - An OpDebugDeclare to associate `spvDebugLocalVar` with `actualHelperVar`\n    // The `actualHelperVar` is used to update the actual value of the variable\n    // at each kIROp_DebugValue instruction.\n    //\n    auto scope = findDebugScope(debugVar);\n    if (!scope)\n        return nullptr;\n\n    bool hasBackingVar = m_mapIRInstToSpvInst.containsKey(debugVar);\n\n    IRBuilder builder(debugVar);\n    builder.setInsertBefore(debugVar);\n\n    auto name = getName(debugVar);\n    auto varType = tryGetPointedToType(&builder, debugVar->getDataType());\n    auto debugType = emitDebugType(varType);\n\n    auto spvDebugLocalVar = emitOpDebugLocalVariable(\n        getSection(SpvLogicalSectionID::ConstantsAndTypes),\n        hasBackingVar ? nullptr : debugVar,\n        m_voidType,\n        getNonSemanticDebugInfoExtInst(),\n        name,\n        debugType,\n        debugVar->getSource(),\n        debugVar->getLine(),\n        debugVar->getCol(),\n        scope,\n        builder.getIntValue(builder.getUIntType(), 0),\n        debugVar->getArgIndex());\n\n    if (hasBackingVar)\n    {\n        emitOpDebugDeclare(\n            parent,\n            nullptr,\n            m_voidType,\n            getNonSemanticDebugInfoExtInst(),\n            spvDebugLocalVar,\n            debugVar,\n            getDwarfExpr(),\n            List<SpvInst*>());\n    }\n\n    return spvDebugLocalVar;\n}\n```\n\n### 3. Backing Variable Creation: `emitDebugVarBackingLocalVarDeclaration` (Lines 3870-3894)\n```cpp\nSpvInst* emitDebugVarBackingLocalVarDeclaration(SpvInstParent* parent, IRDebugVar* debugVar)\n{\n    auto scope = findDebugScope(debugVar);\n    if (!scope)\n        return nullptr;\n\n    IRBuilder builder(debugVar);\n    builder.setInsertBefore(debugVar);\n    auto varType = tryGetPointedToType(&builder, debugVar->getDataType());\n\n    if (!isLegalType(varType))\n        return nullptr;\n\n    IRSizeAndAlignment sizeAlignment;\n    getNaturalSizeAndAlignment(this->m_targetRequest->getOptionSet(), varType, &sizeAlignment);\n    if (sizeAlignment.size != IRSizeAndAlignment::kIndeterminateSize)\n    {\n        auto debugVarPtrType = builder.getPtrType(varType, AddressSpace::Function);\n        auto actualHelperVar =\n            emitOpVariable(parent, debugVar, debugVarPtrType, SpvStorageClassFunction);\n        maybeEmitPointerDecoration(actualHelperVar, debugVar);\n        return actualHelperVar;\n    }\n    return nullptr;\n}\n```\n\n### 4. Debug Value Implementation: `emitDebugValue` (Lines 8179-8238)\n```cpp\nSpvInst* emitDebugValue(SpvInstParent* parent, IRDebugValue* debugValue)\n{\n    // We are asked to update the value for a debug variable.\n    // A debug variable is already emited as a OpDebugVariable +\n    // OpVariable + OpDebugDeclare. We only need to store the new value\n    // into the associated OpVariable. The `debugValue->getDebugVar()` inst\n    // already maps to the `OpVariable` SpvInst, so we just need to emit\n    // code for a store into the subset of the OpVariable with the correct\n    // accesschain defined in the debug value inst.\n    //\n    IRBuilder builder(debugValue);\n    builder.setInsertBefore(debugValue);\n\n    // First we need to check if the debug variable has a backing ordinary\n    // variable. If it doesn't, we can't emit a store.\n    //\n    List<IRInst*> irAccessChain;\n    auto rootVar = getRootAddr(debugValue->getDebugVar(), irAccessChain);\n    SpvInst* spvDebugVar = nullptr;\n    if (!m_mapIRInstToSpvInst.tryGetValue(rootVar, spvDebugVar))\n        return nullptr;\n    if (!spvDebugVar)\n        return nullptr;\n    if (spvDebugVar->opcode != SpvOpVariable)\n    {\n        // If the root variable can't be represented by a normal variable,\n        // try to emit a DebugValue if possible. Usually this means that the variable\n        // represents a shader resource.\n        //\n        // SPIR-V requires the access chain specified in a DebugValue operation to\n        // be fully static. We will skip emitting the debug inst if the access chain\n        // isn't static.\n        //\n        auto type = unwrapAttributedType(debugValue->getDebugVar()->getDataType());\n        List<SpvInst*> accessChain;\n        bool isConstAccessChain =\n            translateIRAccessChain(builder, type, irAccessChain, accessChain);\n\n        if (isConstAccessChain)\n        {\n            return emitOpDebugValue(\n                parent,\n                debugValue,\n                m_voidType,\n                getNonSemanticDebugInfoExtInst(),\n                rootVar,\n                debugValue->getValue(),\n                getDwarfExpr(),\n                accessChain);\n        }\n\n        // Fallback to not emit anything for now.\n        return nullptr;\n    }\n\n    // The ordinary case is the debug variable has a backing ordinary variable.\n    // We can simply emit a store into the backing variable for the DebugValue operation.\n    //\n    return emitStoreMaybeCoherent(parent, debugValue);\n}\n```\n\n### Key Architecture Points:\n\n1. **Two-Stage Process**: Debug variables are handled in two stages:\n   - First, backing `OpVariable` instructions are created early in function emission\n   - Then, `OpDebugLocalVariable` instructions are created when the debug variable IR instruction is processed\n\n2. **SPIR-V Structure**: For each debug variable, three SPIR-V instructions are generated:\n   - `OpDebugLocalVariable` - The debug information\n   - `OpVariable` - The actual storage (backing variable)  \n   - `OpDebugDeclare` - Associates the debug info with the storage\n\n3. **Debug Values**: When debug values are updated (`kIROp_DebugValue`), the system either:\n   - Stores to the backing `OpVariable` if one exists\n   - Emits `OpDebugValue` for resources that can't have backing variables\n\n4. **Section Placement**: Debug-related instructions are placed in the `SpvLogicalSectionID::ConstantsAndTypes` section of the SPIR-V module.\n\nThis implementation enables proper debug information generation in SPIR-V while handling the complexities of different variable types and storage classes."}]}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"a3a19997-4f33-49d9-81e6-d58a077dbf19"}

shaders at similar levels of abstraction, so much of the\n    28→// translation involves one-to-one translation of Slang IR instructions\n    29→// to their SPIR-V equivalents.\n    30→//\n    31→// SPIR-V differs from Slang IR in some key ways, and the SPIR-V\n    32→// specification places many restrictions on how the IR can be encoded.\n    33→// In some cases we will rely on earlier IR passes to convert Slang IR\n    34→// into a form closer to what SPIR-V expects (e.g., by moving all\n    35→// varying entry point parameters to global scope), but other differences\n    36→// will be handled during the translation process.\n    37→//\n    38→// The logic in this file relies on the formal [SPIR-V Specification].\n    39→// When we are making use of or enforcing some property from 43→\n    44→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    45→//\n    46→// > A SPIR-V module is a single linear stream of words.\n    47→\n    48→\n    49→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    50→//\n    51→// > All remaining words are a linear sequence of instructions.\n    52→// > Each instruction is a stream of words\n    53→//\n    54→// After a try to emit instructions directly\n    59→// in one pass as a stream of words, but there are additional\n    60→// constraints placed by the SPIR-V encoding that would make\n    61→// a single-pass strategy very hard, so we don't attempt it.\n    62→//\n    63→// [2.4 Logical Layout of a Module]\n    64→//\n    65→// SPIR-V imposes some global ordering constraints on instructions,\n    66→// such that certain instructions must come before or after others.\n    67→// For example, all `OpCapability` instructions must come before any\n    68→// `OpEntryPoint` instructions.\n    69→//\n    70→// While the SPIR-V spec doesn't use such a term, we will take\n    71→// the enumeration of the ordering in Section 2.4 and use it to\n    72→// define a list of *logical sections* that make up a SPIR-V module.\n    73→\n    74→/// Logical sections of a SPIR-V module.\n    75→enum class SpvLogicalSectionID\n    76→{\n    77→    Capabilities,\n    78→    Extensions,\n    79→ 8235→        // We can simply emit a store into the backing variable for the DebugValue operation.\n  8236→        //\n  8237→        return emitStoreMaybeCoherent(parent, debugValue);\n  8238→    }\n Function Instructions]\n  3582→        //\n  3583→        // > OpFunctionEnd\n  3584→        //\n  3585→        // In the SPIR-V encoding a function is logically the parent of any\n  3586→        // instructions up to a matching `OpFunctionEnd`. In our intermediate\n  3587→        // structure we will make the `OpFunctionEnd` be the last child of\n  3588→        // the `OpFunction`.\n  3589→        //\n  3590→        emitOpFunctionEnd(spvFunc, nullptr);\n  3591→\n  3592→        // We will emit any decorations pertinent to the function to the\n  3593→        // appropriate section of the module.\n  3594→        //\n  3595→        emitDecorations(irFunc, getID(spvFunc));\n  3596→\n  3597→        return spvFunc;\n  3598→    }\n  3599→\n  3600→    /// Check if a block is a loop's target block.\n  3601→    bool 16→#include \"spirv/unified1/spirv.h\"\n    17→\n    18→#include <type_traits>\n    19→\n    20→namespace Slang\n    21→{\n    22→\n    23→// Our goal in this file is to convert a module in the Slang IR over to an\n    24→// equivalent module in the SPIR-V intermediate language.\n    25→//\n    26→// The Slang IR is (intentionally) similar to SPIR-V in many ways, and both\n    27→// can represent shaders at similar levels of abstraction, so much of the\n    28→// translation involves one-to-one translation of Slang IR instructions\n    29→// to their SPIR-V equivalents.\n    30→//\n    31→// SPIR-V differs from Slang IR in some key ways, and the SPIR-V\n    32→// specification places many restrictions on how the IR can be encoded.\n    33→// In some cases we will rely on earlier IR passes to convert Slang IR\n    34→// into a form closer to what SPIR-V expects (e.g., by moving all\n    35→// varying entry point parameters to global scope), but other differences\n    36→// will be handled during the translation process.\n    37→//\n    38→// The logic in this file relies on the formal [SPIR-V Specification].\n    39→// When we are making use of or enforcing some property from the spec,\n    40→// we will try to refer to the relevant section in comments.\n    41→//\n    42→// [SPIR-V Specification]: https://www.khronos.org/registry/spir-v/specs/unified1/SPIRV.html\n    43→\n    44→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    45→//\n    46→// > A SPIR-V module is a single linear stream of words.\n    47→\n    48→\n    49→// [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n    50→//\n    51→// > All remaining words are a linear sequence of instructions.\n    52→// > Each instruction is a stream of words\n    53→//\n    54→// After a fixed-size header, the contents of a SPIR-V module\n    55→// is just a flat sequence of instructions, each of which is\n    56→// just a sequence of words.\n    57→//\n    58→// In principle we could try to emit instructions directly\n    59→// in one pass as a stream of words, but there are additional\n    60→// constraints placed by the SPIR-V encoding that would make\n    61→// a single-pass strategy very hard, so we don't attempt it.\n    62→//\n    63→// [2.4 Logical Layout of a Module]\n    64→//\n    65→// SPIR-V imposes some global ordering constraints on instructions,\n    66→// such that certain instructions must come before or after others.\n    67→// For example, all `OpCapability` instructions must come before any\n    68→// `OpEntryPoint` instructions.\n    69→//\n    70→// While the SPIR-V spec doesn't use such a term, we will take\n    71→// the enumeration of the ordering in Section 2.4 and use it to\n    72→// define a list of *logical sections* that make up a SPIR-V module.\n    73→\n    74→/// Logical sections of a SPIR-V module.\n    75→enum class SpvLogicalSectionID\n    76→{\n    77→    Capabilities,\n    78→    Extensions,\n    79→    ExtIntInstImports,\n    80→    MemoryModel,\n    81→    EntryPoints,\n    82→    ExecutionModes,\n    83→    DebugStringsAndSource,\n    84→    DebugNames,\n    85→    Annotations,\n    86→    ConstantsAndTypes,\n    87→    GlobalVariables,\n    88→    FunctionDeclarations,\n    89→    FunctionDefinitions,\n    90→\n    91→    Count,\n    92→};\n    93→\n    94→// The registered id for the Slang compiler.\n    95→static const uint32_t kSPIRVSlangCompilerId = 40 << 16;\n    96→\n    97→// While the SPIR-V module is nominally (according to the spec) just\n    98→// a flat sequence of instructions, in practice some of the instructions\n    99→// are logically in a parent/child relationship.\n   100→//\n   101→// In particular, functions \"own\" the instructions between an `OpFunction`\n   102→// and the matching `OpFunctionEnd`. We can also think of basic\n   103→// blocks within a function as owning the instructions between\n   104→// an `OpLabel` (which represents the bloc) and the next label\n   105→// or the end of the function.\n   106→//\n   107→// Furthermore, the common case is SPIR-V is that an instruction\n   108→// that defines some value must appear before any instruction\n   109→// that uses that value as an operand. This property is often true\n   110→// in a Slang IR module, but isn't strictly enforced for things at\n   111→// the global scope.\n   112→//\n   113→// To deal with the above issues, our strategy will be to emit\n   114→// SPIR-V instructions into a lightweight intermediate structure\n   115→// that simplifies dealing with ordering constraiints on\n   116→// instructions.\n   117→//\n   118→// We will start by forward-declaring the type we will\n   119→// use to represent instructions:\n   120→//\n   121→struct SpvInst;\n   122→\n   123→// Next, we will define a base type that can serve as a parent\n   124→// to SPIR-V instructions. Both the logical sections defined\n   125→// earlier and instructions such as functions will be used\n   126→// as parents.\n   127→\n   128→/// Base type for SPIR-V instructions and logical sections of a module\n   129→///\n   130→/// Holds and supports appending to a list of child instructions.\n   131→struct SpvInstParent\n   132→{\n   133→public:\n   134→    /// Add an instruction to the end of the list of children\n   135→    void addInst(SpvInst* inst);\n   136→\n   137→    /// Dump all children, recursively, to a flattened list of SPIR-V words\n   138→    void dumpTo(List<SpvWord>& ioWords);\n   139→\n   140→    /// The first child, if any.\n   141→    SpvInst* m_firstChild = nullptr;\n   142→\n   143→    /// A pointer to the null pointer at the end of the linked list.\n   144→    ///\n   145→    /// If the list of children is empty this points to `m_firstChild`,\n   146→    /// while if it is non-empty it points to the `nextSibling` field\n   147→    /// of the last instruction.\n   148→    ///\n   149→    SpvInst* m_lastChild = nullptr;\n   150→};\n   151→\n   152→// A SPIR-V instruction is then (in the general case) a potential\n   153→// parent to other instructions.\n   154→\n   155→/// A type to represent a SPIR-V instruction to be emitted.\n   156→///\n   157→/// This type alows the instruction to be built up across\n   158→/// multiple steps in a mutable fashion.\n   159→///\n   160→struct SpvInst : SpvInstParent\n   161→{\n   162→    // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   163→    //\n   164→    // > Each instruction is a stream of words\n   165→    //\n   166→    // > Opcode: The 16 high-order bits are the WordCount of the instruction.\n   167→    // >         The 16 low-order bits are the opcode enumerant.\n   168→    //\n   169→    // We will store the \"opcode enumerant\" directly in our\n   170→    // intermediate structure, and compute the word count on\n   171→    // the fly when writing an instruction to an output buffer.\n   172→\n   173→    /// The SPIR-V opcode for the instruction\n   174→    SpvOp opcode;\n   175→\n   176→    // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   177→    //\n   178→    // > Optional instruction type <id> (presence determined by opcode)\n   179→    // > Optional instruction Result <id> (presence determined by opcode)\n   180→    // > Operand 1 (if needed)\n   181→    // > Operand 2 (if needed)\n   182→    // > ...\n   183→    //\n   184→    // We represent the remaining words of the instruction (after\n   185→    // the opcode word) as an undifferentiated array. Any code\n   186→    // that encodes an instruction is responsible for knowing the\n   187→    // opcode-specific data that is required.\n   188→    //\n   189→    // Our code does not need to process instruction operands after\n   190→    // they have been written into a `SpvInst`. If we ever had\n   191→    // cases where we needed to do post-processing, then we would\n   192→    // need to store a more refined representation here.\n   193→\n   194→    /// The additional words of the instruction after the opcode\n   195→    SpvWord* operandWords = nullptr;\n   196→    /// The amount of operand words\n   197→    uint32_t operandWordsCount = 0;\n   198→\n   199→    // We will store the instructions in a given `SpvInstParent`\n   200→    // using an intrusive linked list.\n   201→\n   202→    SpvInstParent* parent = nullptr;\n   203→\n   204→    /// The next instruction in the same `SpvInstParent`\n   205→    SpvInst* nextSibling = nullptr;\n   206→\n   207→    SpvInst* prevSibling = nullptr;\n   208→\n   209→    /// The result <id> produced by this instruction, or zero if it has no result.\n   210→    SpvWord id = 0;\n   211→\n   212→    /// Dump the instruction (and any children, recursively) into the flat array of SPIR-V words.\n   213→    void dumpTo(List<SpvWord>& ioWords)\n   214→    {\n   215→        // [2.2: Terms]\n   216→        //\n   217→        // > Word Count: The complete number of words taken by an instruction,\n   218→        // > including the word holding the word count and opcode, and any optional\n   219→        // > operands. An instruction’s word count is the total space taken by the instruction.\n   220→        //\n   221→        SpvWord wordCount = 1 + SpvWord(operandWordsCount);\n   222→\n   223→        // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   224→        //\n   225→        // > Opcode: The 16 high-order bits are the WordCount of the instruction.\n   226→        // >         The 16 low-order bits are the opcode enumerant.\n   227→        //\n   228→        ioWords.add(wordCount << 16 | opcode);\n   229→\n   230→        // The operand words simply follow the opcode word.\n   231→        //\n   232→        ioWords.addRange(operandWords, operandWordsCount);\n   233→\n   234→        // In our representation choice, the children of a\n   235→        // parent instruction will always follow the encoded\n   236→        // words of a parent:\n   237→        //\n   238→        // * The instructions inside a function always follow the `OpFunction`\n   239→        // * The instructions inside a block always follow the `OpLabel`\n   240→        //\n   241→        SpvInstParent::dumpTo(ioWords);\n   242→    }\n   243→\n   244→    void removeFromParent()\n   245→    {\n   246→        auto oldParent = parent;\n   247→\n   248→        // If we don't currently have a parent, then\n   249→        // we are doing fine.\n   250→        if (!oldParent)\n   251→            return;\n   252→\n   253→        auto pp = prevSibling;\n   254→        auto nn = nextSibling;\n   255→\n   256→        if (pp)\n   257→        {\n   258→            SLANG_ASSERT(pp->parent == oldParent);\n   259→            pp->nextSibling = nn;\n   260→        }\n   261→        else\n   262→        {\n   263→            oldParent->m_firstChild = nn;\n   264→        }\n   265→\n   266→        if (nn)\n   267→        {\n   268→            SLANG_ASSERT(nn->parent == oldParent);\n   269→            nn->prevSibling = pp;\n   270→        }\n   271→        else\n   272→        {\n   273→            oldParent->m_lastChild = pp;\n   274→        }\n   275→\n   276→        prevSibling = nullptr;\n   277→        nextSibling = nullptr;\n   278→        parent = nullptr;\n   279→    }\n   280→};\n   281→\n   282→/// A logical section of a SPIR-V module\n   283→struct SpvLogicalSection : SpvInstParent\n   284→{\n   285→};\n   286→\n   287→// Now that we've filled in the definition of `SpvInst`, we can\n   288→// go back and define the key operations on `SpvInstParent`.\n   289→\n   290→void SpvInstParent::addInst(SpvInst* inst)\n   291→{\n   292→    SLANG_ASSERT(inst);\n   293→    SLANG_ASSERT(!inst->nextSibling);\n   294→\n   295→    if (m_firstChild == nullptr)\n   296→    {\n   297→        m_firstChild = m_lastChild = inst;\n   298→        return;\n   299→    }\n   300→\n   301→    // The user shouldn't be trying to add multiple instructions at once.\n   302→    // If they really want that then they probably wanted to give `inst`\n   303→    // some children.\n   304→    //\n   305→    m_lastChild->nextSibling = inst;\n   306→    inst->prevSibling = m_lastChild;\n   307→    inst->parent = this;\n   308→    m_lastChild = inst;\n   309→}\n   310→\n   311→void SpvInstParent::dumpTo(List<SpvWord>& ioWords)\n   312→{\n   313→    for (auto child = m_firstChild; child; child = child->nextSibling)\n   314→    {\n   315→        child->dumpTo(ioWords);\n   316→    }\n   317→}\n   318→\n   319→/// The context for inlining a SPV assembly snippet.\n   320→struct SpvSnippetEmitContext\n   321→{\n   322→    SpvInst* resultType;\n   323→    IRType* irResultType;\n   324→    // True if resultType is float or vector of float.\n   325→    bool isResultTypeFloat;\n   326→    // True if resultType is signed.\n   327→    bool isResultTypeSigned;\n   328→    List<SpvWord> argumentIds;\n   329→};\n   330→\n   331→// A structure which can hold an integer literal, either one word or several\n   332→struct SpvLiteralInteger\n   333→{\n   334→    static SpvLiteralInteger from32(int32_t value) { return from32(uint32_t(value)); }\n   335→    static SpvLiteralInteger from32(uint32_t value) { return SpvLiteralInteger{{value}}; }\n   336→    static SpvLiteralInteger from64(int64_t value) { return from64(uint64_t(value)); }\n   337→    static SpvLiteralInteger from64(uint64_t value)\n   338→    {\n   339→        return SpvLiteralInteger{{SpvWord(value), SpvWord(value >> 32)}};\n   340→    }\n   341→    List<SpvWord> value; // Words, stored low words to high (TODO, SmallArray or something here)\n   342→};\n   343→\n   344→// A structure which can hold bitwise literal, either one word or several\n   345→struct SpvLiteralBits\n   346→{\n   347→    static SpvLiteralBits from32(uint32_t value) { return SpvLiteralBits{{value}}; }\n   348→    static SpvLiteralBits from64(uint64_t value)\n   349→    {\n   350→        return SpvLiteralBits{{SpvWord(value), SpvWord(value >> 32)}};\n   351→    }\n   352→    static SpvLiteralBits fromUnownedStringSlice(UnownedStringSlice text)\n   353→    {\n   354→        SpvLiteralBits result;\n   355→\n   356→        // [Section 2.2.1 : Instructions]\n   357→        //\n   358→        // > Literal String: A nul-terminated stream of characters consuming\n   359→        // > an integral number of words. The character set is Unicode in the\n   360→        // > UTF-8 encoding scheme. The UTF-8 octets (8-bit bytes) are packed\n   361→        // > four per word, following the little-endian convention (i.e., the\n   362→        // > first octet is in the lowest-order 8 bits of the word).\n   363→        // > The final word contains the string’s nul-termination character (0), and\n   364→        // > all contents past the end of the string in the final word are padded with 0.\n   365→\n   366→        // First work out the amount of words we'll need\n   367→        const Index textCount = text.getLength();\n   368→        // Calculate the minimum amount of bytes needed - which needs to include terminating 0\n   369→        const Index minByteCount = textCount + 1;\n   370→        // Calculate the amount of words including padding if necessary\n   371→        const Index wordCount = (minByteCount + 3) >> 2;\n   372→\n   373→        // Make space on the operand stack, keeping the free space start in operandStartIndex\n   374→        result.value.setCount(wordCount);\n   375→\n   376→        // Set dst to the start of the operand memory\n   377→        char* dst = (char*)(result.value.getBuffer());\n   378→\n   379→        // Copy the text\n   380→        SLANG_ASSUME(textCount >= 0);\n   381→        memcpy(dst, text.begin(), textCount);\n   382→\n   383→        // Set terminating 0, and remaining buffer 0s\n   384→        memset(dst + textCount, 0, wordCount * sizeof(SpvWord) - textCount);\n   385→\n   386→        return result;\n   387→    }\n   388→    List<SpvWord> value; // Words, stored low words to high (TODO, SmallArray or something here)\n   389→};\n   390→\n   391→// As a convenience, there are often cases where\n   392→// we will want to emit all of the operands of some\n   393→// IR instruction as <id> operands of a SPIR-V\n   394→// instruction. This is handy in cases where the\n   395→// Slang IR and SPIR-V instructions agree on the\n   396→// number, order, and meaning of their operands.\n   397→/// Helper type for emitting all the operands of the current IR instruction\n   398→struct OperandsOf\n   399→{\n   400→    OperandsOf(IRInst* irInst)\n   401→        : irInst(irInst)\n   402→    {\n   403→    }\n   404→\n   405→    IRInst* irInst = nullptr;\n   406→};\n   407→\n   408→/// Helper type for not emitting an operand in this position\n   409→struct SkipThisOptionalOperand\n   410→{\n   411→};\n   412→\n   413→template<typename T>\n   414→struct OptionalOperand\n   415→{\n   416→    static_assert(std::is_trivial_v<T>);\n   417→    OptionalOperand(SkipThisOptionalOperand)\n   418→        : present(false)\n   419→    {\n   420→    }\n   421→    OptionalOperand(T value)\n   422→        : present(true), value(value)\n   423→    {\n   424→    }\n   425→    bool present;\n   426→    T value;\n   427→};\n   428→\n   429→template<typename T>\n   430→OptionalOperand<T> nullOptionOperand()\n   431→{\n   432→    return OptionalOperand<T>{false};\n   433→}\n   434→\n   435→template<typename T>\n   436→OptionalOperand<T> someOptionOperand(T t)\n   437→{\n   438→    return OptionalOperand<T>{true, t};\n   439→}\n   440→\n   441→template<typename T>\n   442→constexpr bool isPlural = false;\n   443→template<typename T>\n   444→constexpr bool isPlural<List<T>> = true;\n   445→template<typename T>\n   446→constexpr bool isPlural<IROperandList<T>> = true;\n   447→template<typename T, Index N>\n   448→constexpr bool isPlural<Array<T, N>> = true;\n   449→template<>\n   450→constexpr bool isPlural<OperandsOf> = true;\n   451→template<>\n   452→constexpr bool isPlural<IRUse*> = true;\n   453→template<typename T>\n   454→constexpr bool isSingular = !isPlural<T>;\n   455→\n   456→\n   457→// Now that we've defined the intermediate data structures we will\n   458→// use to represent SPIR-V code during emission, we will move on\n   459→// to defining the main context type that will drive SPIR-V\n   460→// code generation.\n   461→\n   462→/// Context used for translating a Slang IR module to SPIR-V\n   463→struct SPIRVEmitContext : public SourceEmitterBase, public SPIRVEmitSharedContext\n   464→{\n   465→    /// The Slang IR module being translated\n   466→    IRModule* m_irModule;\n   467→\n   468→    // [2.2: Terms]\n   469→    //\n   470→    // > <id>: A numerical name; the name used to refer to an object, a type,\n   471→    // > a function, a label, etc. An <id> always consumes one word.\n   472→    // > The <id>s defined by a module obey SSA.\n   473→    //\n   474→    // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   475→    //\n   476→    // > Bound; where all <id>s in this module are guaranteed to satisfy\n   477→    // > 0 < id < Bound\n   478→    // > Bound should be small, smaller is better, with all <id> in a module being densely packed\n   479→    // and near 0.\n   480→    //\n   481→    // Instructions will be referred to by their <id>s.\n   482→    // We need to generate <id>s for instructions, and also\n   483→    // compute the \"bound\" value that will be stored in\n   484→    // the module header.\n   485→    //\n   486→    // We will use a single counter and allocate <id>s\n   487→    // on demand. There may be some slop where we allocate\n   488→    // an <id> for something that never gets referenced,\n   489→    // but we expect the amount of slop to be small (and\n   490→    // it can be cleaned up by other tools/passes).\n   491→\n   492→    /// The next destination `<id>` to allocate.\n   493→    SpvWord m_nextID = 1;\n   494→\n   495→    OrderedDictionary<SpvInst*, IRPtrTypeBase*> m_forwardDeclaredPointers;\n   496→\n   497→    SpvInst* m_nullDwarfExpr = nullptr;\n   498→\n   499→    // A hash set to prevent redecorating the same spv inst.\n   500→    HashSet<SpvId> m_decoratedSpvInsts;\n   501→\n   502→    SpvAddressingModel m_addressingMode = SpvAddressingModelLogical;\n   503→\n   504→    // We will store the logical sections of the SPIR-V module\n   505→    // in a single array so that we can easily look up a\n   506→    // section by its `SpvLogicalSectionID`.\n   507→\n   508→    /// The logical sections of the SPIR-V module\n   509→    SpvLogicalSection m_sections[int(SpvLogicalSectionID::Count)];\n   510→\n   511→    /// Get a logical section based on its `SpvLogicalSectionID`\n   512→    SpvLogicalSection* getSection(SpvLogicalSectionID id) { return &m_sections[int(id)]; }\n   513→\n   514→    // At the end of emission we need a single linear stream of words,\n   515→    // so we will eventually flatten `m_sections` into a single array.\n   516→\n   517→    /// The final array of SPIR-V words that defines the encoded module\n   518→    List<SpvWord> m_words;\n   519→\n   520→    /// Emit the concrete words that make up the binary SPIR-V module.\n   521→    ///\n   522→    /// This function fills in `m_words` based on the data in `m_sections`.\n   523→    /// This function should only be called once.\n   524→    ///\n   525→    void emitPhysicalLayout()\n   526→    {\n   527→        // [2.3: Physical Layout of a SPIR-V Module and Instruction]\n   528→        //\n   529→        // > Magic Number\n   530→        //\n   531→        m_words.add(SpvMagicNumber);\n   532→\n   533→        // > Version nuumber\n   534→        //\n   535→        m_words.add(m_spvVersion);\n   536→\n   537→        // > Generator's magic number.\n   538→        //\n   539→        m_words.add(kSPIRVSlangCompilerId);\n   540→\n   541→        // > Bound\n   542→        //\n   543→        // As described above, we use `m_nextID` to allocate\n   544→        // <id>s, so its value when we are done emitting code\n   545→        // can serve as the bound.\n   546→        //\n   547→        m_words.add(m_nextID);\n   548→\n   549→        // > 0 (Reserved for instruction schema, if needed.)\n   550→        //\n   551→        m_words.add(0);\n   552→\n   553→        // > First word of instruction stream\n   554→        // > All remaining words are a linear sequence of instructions.\n   555→        //\n   556→        // Once we are done emitting the header, we emit all\n   557→        // the instructions in our logical sections.\n   558→        //\n   559→        for (int ii = 0; ii < int(SpvLogicalSectionID::Count); ++ii)\n   560→        {\n   561→            m_sections[ii].dumpTo(m_words);\n   562→        }\n   563→    }\n   564→\n   565→    // We will often need to refer to an instrcition by its\n   566→    // <id>, given only the Slang IR instruction that represents\n   567→    // it (e.g., when it is used as an operand of another\n   568→    // instruction).\n   569→    //\n   570→    // To that end we will keep a map of instructions that\n   571→    // have been emitted, where a Slang IR instruction maps\n   572→    // to the corresponding SPIR-V instruction.\n   573→\n   574→    /// Map a Slang IR instruction to the corresponding SPIR-V instruction\n   575→    Dictionary<IRInst*, SpvInst*> m_mapIRInstToSpvInst;\n   576→\n   577→    // Sometimes we need to reserve an ID for an `IRInst` without actually\n   578→    // emitting it. We use `m_mapIRInstToSpvID` to hold all reserved SpvIDs.\n   579→    // Use `getIRInstSpvID` to obtain an SpvID for an `IRInst` if the\n   580→    // `IRInst` may not have been emitted.\n   581→    Dictionary<IRInst*, SpvWord> m_mapIRInstToSpvID;\n   582→\n   583→    // Map a Slang IR instruction to the corresponding SPIR-V debug instruction.\n   584→    Dictionary<IRInst*, SpvInst*> m_mapIRInstToSpvDebugInst;\n   585→\n   586→    /// Register that `irInst` maps to `spvInst`\n   587→    void registerInst(IRInst* irInst, SpvInst* spvInst)\n   588→    {\n   589→        m_mapIRInstToSpvInst.add(irInst, spvInst);\n   590→\n   591→        // If we have reserved an SpvID for `irInst`, make sure to use it.\n   592→        SpvWord reservedID = 0;\n   593→        m_mapIRInstToSpvID.tryGetValue(irInst, reservedID);\n   594→\n   595→        if (reservedID)\n   596→        {\n   597→            SLANG_ASSERT(spvInst->id == 0);\n   598→            spvInst->id = reservedID;\n   599→        }\n   600→    }\n   601→\n   602→    /// Register that `irInst` has debug info represented by `spvDebugInst`.\n   603→    void registerDebugInst(IRInst* irInst, SpvInst* spvDebugInst)\n   604→    {\n   605→        m_mapIRInstToSpvDebugInst.add(irInst, spvDebugInst);\n   606→    }\n   607→\n   608→    SpvInst* findDebugScope(IRInst* inst)\n   609→    {\n   610→        for (auto parent = inst; parent; parent = parent->getParent())\n   611→        {\n   612→            if (!as<IRFunc>(parent) && !as<IRModuleInst>(parent))\n   613→                continue;\n   614→\n   615→            SpvInst* spvInst = nullptr;\n   616→            if (m_mapIRInstToSpvDebugInst.tryGetValue(parent, spvInst))\n   617→                return spvInst;\n   618→        }\n   619→        return nullptr;\n   620→    }\n   621→\n   622→    /// Get or reserve a SpvID for an IR value.\n   623→    SpvWord getIRInstSpvID(IRInst* inst)\n   624→    {\n   625→        // If we have already emitted an SpvInst for `inst`, return its ID.\n   626→        SpvInst* spvInst = nullptr;\n   627→        if (m_mapIRInstToSpvInst.tryGetValue(inst, spvInst))\n   628→            return getID(spvInst);\n   629→        // Check if we have reserved an ID for `inst`.\n   630→        SpvWord result = 0;\n   631→        if (m_mapIRInstToSpvID.tryGetValue(inst, result))\n   632→            return result;\n   633→        // Otherwise, reserve a new ID for inst, and register it in `m_mapIRInstToSpvID`.\n   634→        result = m_nextID;\n   635→        ++m_nextID;\n   636→        m_mapIRInstToSpvID[inst] = result;\n   637→        return result;\n   638→    }\n   639→\n   640→    // When we are emitting an instruction that can produce\n   641→    // a result, we will allocate an <id> to it so that other\n   642→    // instructions can refer to it.\n   643→    //\n   644→    // We will allocate <id>s on emand as they are needed.\n   645→\n   646→    SpvWord freshID() { return m_nextID++; }\n   647→\n   648→    /// Get the <id> for `inst`, or assign one if it doesn't have one yet\n   649→    SpvWord getID(SpvInst* inst)\n   650→    {\n   651→        auto id = inst->id;\n   652→        if (!id)\n   653→        {\n   654→            id = freshID();\n   655→            inst->id = id;\n   656→        }\n   657→        return id;\n   658→    }\n   659→\n   660→    // We will build up `SpvInst`s in a stateful fashion,\n   661→    // mostly for convenience. We could in theory compute\n   662→    // the number of words each instruction needs, then allocate\n   663→    // the words, then fill them in, but that would make the\n   664→    // emit logic more complicated and we'd like to keep it simple\n   665→    // until we are sure performance is an issue.\n   666→    //\n   667→    // Emitting an instruction starts with picking the opcode\n   668→    // and allocating the `SpvInst`.\n   669→\n   670→    // Holds a stack of instructions operands *BEFORE* they added to the instruction.\n   671→    List<SpvWord> m_operandStack;\n   672→    // The current instruction being constructed. Cannot add operands unless it\n   673→    // is set, or we are peeking at some operands to see if we have them memoized\n   674→    SpvInst* m_currentInst = nullptr;\n   675→    bool m_peekingOperands = false;\n   676→\n   677→    // Operands can only be added when inside of a InstConstructScope or...\n   678→    struct InstConstructScope\n   679→    {\n   680→        SLANG_FORCE_INLINE operator SpvInst*() const { return m_inst; }\n   681→\n   682→        InstConstructScope(SPIRVEmitContext* context, SpvOp opcode, IRInst* irInst = nullptr)\n   683→            : m_context(context)\n   684→        {\n   685→            m_context->_beginInst(opcode, irInst, *this);\n   686→        }\n   687→        ~InstConstructScope() { m_context->_endInst(*this); }\n   688→\n   689→        SpvInst* m_inst;             ///< The instruction associated with this scope\n   690→        SPIRVEmitContext* m_context; ///< The context\n   691→        SpvInst* m_previousInst;     ///< The previously live inst\n   692→        Index m_operandsStartIndex;  ///< The start index for operands of m_inst\n   693→    };\n   694→\n   695→    // ...If we're speculatively adding them to see if we have a memoized results\n   696→    struct OperandMemoizeScope\n   697→    {\n   698→        OperandMemoizeScope(SPIRVEmitContext* context)\n   699→            : m_context(context)\n   700→        {\n   701→            m_tmpOperandStack.swapWith(m_context->m_operandStack);\n   702→            std::swap(m_tmpPeeking, m_context->m_peekingOperands);\n   703→            std::swap(m_tmpInst, m_context->m_currentInst);\n   704→        }\n   705→        ~OperandMemoizeScope()\n   706→        {\n   707→            std::swap(m_tmpInst, m_context->m_currentInst);\n   708→            std::swap(m_tmpPeeking, m_context->m_peekingOperands);\n   709→            m_tmpOperandStack.swapWith(m_context->m_operandStack);\n   710→        }\n   711→\n   712→        SPIRVEmitContext* m_context;\n   713→        List<SpvWord> m_tmpOperandStack;\n   714→        bool m_tmpPeeking = true;\n   715→        SpvInst* m_tmpInst = nullptr;\n   716→    };\n   717→\n   718→    /// Holds memory for instructions and operands.\n   719→    MemoryArena m_memoryArena;\n   720→\n   721→    /// Begin emitting an instruction with the given SPIR-V `opcode`.\n   722→    ///\n   723→    /// If `irInst` is non-null, then the resulting SPIR-V instruction\n   724→    /// will be registered as corresponding to `irInst`.\n   725→    ///\n   726→    /// The created instruction is stored in m_currentInst.\n   727→    ///\n   728→    /// Should not typically be called directly use InstConstructScope to scope construction\n   729→    void _beginInst(SpvOp opcode, IRInst* irInst, InstConstructScope& ioScope)\n   730→    {\n   731→        SLANG_ASSERT(this == ioScope.m_context);\n   732→\n   733→        // Allocate the instruction\n   734→        auto spvInst = new (m_memoryArena.allocate(sizeof(SpvInst))) SpvInst();\n   735→        spvInst->opcode = opcode;\n   736→\n   737→        if (irInst)\n   738→        {\n   739→            registerInst(irInst, spvInst);\n   740→        }\n   741→\n   742→        // Set up the scope\n   743→        ioScope.m_inst = spvInst;\n   744→        ioScope.m_previousInst = m_currentInst;\n   745→        ioScope.m_operandsStartIndex = m_operandStack.getCount();\n   746→\n   747→        // Set the current instruction\n   748→        m_currentInst = spvInst;\n   749→    }\n   750→\n   751→    /// End emitting an instruction\n   752→    /// Should not typically be called directly use InstConstructScope to scope construction\n   753→    void _endInst(const InstConstructScope& scope)\n   754→    {\n   755→        SLANG_ASSERT(scope.m_inst == m_currentInst);\n   756→\n   757→        const Index operandsStartIndex = scope.m_operandsStartIndex;\n   758→        // Work out how many operands were added\n   759→        const Index operandsCount = m_operandStack.getCount() - operandsStartIndex;\n   760→\n   761→\n   762→        if (operandsCount)\n   763→        {\n   764→            // Allocate the operands\n   765→            m_currentInst->operandWords = m_memoryArena.allocateAndCopyArray(\n   766→                m_operandStack.getBuffer() + operandsStartIndex,\n   767→                operandsCount);\n   768→            // Set the count\n   769→            m_currentInst->operandWordsCount = uint32_t(operandsCount);\n   770→        }\n   771→\n   772→        // Make the previous inst active\n   773→        m_currentInst = scope.m_previousInst;\n   774→        // Reset the operand stack\n   775→        m_operandStack.setCount(operandsStartIndex);\n   776→    }\n   777→\n   778→    SpvOp _specConstantOpcodeConvert(IROp irOpCode, IRBasicType* basicType)\n   779→    {\n   780→        SpvOp opCode = SpvOpUndef;\n   781→        opCode = _arithmeticOpCodeConvert(irOpCode, basicType);\n   782→        if (opCode == SpvOpUndef)\n   783→        {\n   784→            switch (irOpCode)\n   785→            {\n   786→            case kIROp_IntCast:\n   787→                return SpvOpUConvert;\n   788→            case kIROp_FloatCast:\n   789→                return SpvOpFConvert;\n   790→            default:\n   791→                break;\n   792→            }\n   793→            return opCode;\n   794→        }\n   795→        return opCode;\n   796→    }\n   797→\n   798→    SpvOp _arithmeticOpCodeConvert(IROp irOpCode, IRBasicType* basicType)\n   799→    {\n   800→        bool isFloatingPoint = false;\n   801→        bool isBool = false;\n   802→        switch (basicType->getBaseType())\n   803→        {\n   804→        case BaseType::Float:\n   805→        case BaseType::Double:\n   806→        case BaseType::Half:\n   807→            isFloatingPoint = true;\n   808→            break;\n   809→        case BaseType::Bool:\n   810→            isBool = true;\n   811→            break;\n   812→        default:\n   813→            break;\n   814→        }\n   815→        bool isSigned = isSignedType(basicType);\n   816→        SpvOp opCode = SpvOpUndef;\n   817→        switch (irOpCode)\n   818→        {\n   819→        case kIROp_Add:\n   820→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFAdd : SpvOpIAdd;\n   821→            break;\n   822→        case kIROp_Sub:\n   823→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFSub : SpvOpISub;\n   824→            break;\n   825→        case kIROp_Mul:\n   826→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFMul : SpvOpIMul;\n   827→            break;\n   828→        case kIROp_Div:\n   829→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFDiv : isSigned ? SpvOpSDiv : SpvOpUDiv;\n   830→            break;\n   831→        case kIROp_IRem:\n   832→            opCode = isSigned ? SpvOpSRem : SpvOpUMod;\n   833→            break;\n   834→        case kIROp_FRem:\n   835→            opCode = SpvOpFRem;\n   836→            break;\n   837→        case kIROp_Less:\n   838→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFOrdLessThan\n   839→                     : isSigned      ? SpvOpSLessThan\n   840→                                     : SpvOpULessThan;\n   841→            break;\n   842→        case kIROp_Leq:\n   843→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFOrdLessThanEqual\n   844→                     : isSigned      ? SpvOpSLessThanEqual\n   845→                                     : SpvOpULessThanEqual;\n   846→            break;\n   847→        case kIROp_Eql:\n   848→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFOrdEqual : isBool ? SpvOpLogicalEqual : SpvOpIEqual;\n   849→            break;\n   850→        case kIROp_Neq:\n   851→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFUnordNotEqual\n   852→                     : isBool        ? SpvOpLogicalNotEqual\n   853→                                     : SpvOpINotEqual;\n   854→            break;\n   855→        case kIROp_Geq:\n   856→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFOrdGreaterThanEqual\n   857→                     : isSigned      ? SpvOpSGreaterThanEqual\n   858→                                     : SpvOpUGreaterThanEqual;\n   859→            break;\n   860→        case kIROp_Greater:\n   861→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFOrdGreaterThan\n   862→                     : isSigned      ? SpvOpSGreaterThan\n   863→                                     : SpvOpUGreaterThan;\n   864→            break;\n   865→        case kIROp_Neg:\n   866→            opCode = isFloatingPoint ? SpvOpFNegate : SpvOpSNegate;\n   867→            break;\n   868→        case kIROp_And:\n   869→            opCode = SpvOpLogicalAnd;\n   870→            break;\n   871→        case kIROp_Or:\n   872→            opCode = SpvOpLogicalOr;\n   873→            break;\n   874→        case kIROp_Not:\n   875→            opCode = SpvOpLogicalNot;\n   876→            break;\n   877→        case kIROp_BitAnd:\n   878→            if (isBool)\n   879→                opCode = SpvOpLogicalAnd;\n   880→            else\n   881→                opCode = SpvOpBitwiseAnd;\n   882→            break;\n   883→        case kIROp_BitOr:\n   884→            if (isBool)\n   885→                opCode = SpvOpLogicalOr;\n   886→            else\n   887→                opCode = SpvOpBitwiseOr;\n   888→            break;\n   889→        case kIROp_BitXor:\n   890→            if (isBool)\n   891→                opCode = SpvOpLogicalNotEqual;\n   892→            else\n   893→                opCode = SpvOpBitwiseXor;\n   894→            break;\n   895→        case kIROp_BitNot:\n   896→            if (isBool)\n   897→                opCode = SpvOpLogicalNot;\n   898→            else\n   899→                opCode = SpvOpNot;\n   900→            break;\n   901→        case kIROp_Rsh:\n   902→            opCode = isSigned ? SpvOpShiftRightArithmetic : SpvOpShiftRightLogical;\n   903→            break;\n   904→        case kIROp_Lsh:\n   905→            opCode = SpvOpShiftLeftLogical;\n   906→            break;\n   907→        }\n   908→        return opCode;\n   909→    }\n   910→    /// Ensure that an instruction has been emitted\n   911→    SpvInst* ensureInst(IRInst* irInst)\n   912→    {\n   913→        SpvInst* spvInst = nullptr;\n   914→        if (!m_mapIRInstToSpvInst.tryGetValue(irInst, spvInst))\n   915→        {\n   916→            // If the `irInst` hasn't already been emitted,\n   917→            // then we will assume that is is a global instruction\n   918→            // (a constant, type, function, etc.) and we should make\n   919→            // sure it gets emitted now.\n   920→            //\n   921→            // Note: this step means that emitting an instruction\n   922→            // can be re-entrant/recursive. Because we emit the SPIR-V\n   923→            // words for an instruction into an intermediate structure\n   924→            // we don't have to worry about the re-entrancy causing\n   925→            // the ordering of instruction words to be interleaved.\n   926→            //\n   927→            spvInst = emitGlobalInst(irInst);\n   928→        }\n   929→        return spvInst;\n   930→    }\n   931→\n   932→    // Whilst an instruction has been created, we append the operand\n   933→    // words to it with `emitOperand`. There are a few different\n   934→    // case of operands that we handle.\n   935→    //\n   936→    // The simplest case is when an instruction takes an operand\n   937→    // that is just a literal SPIR-V word.\n   938→\n   939→    /// Emit a literal `word` as an operand to the current instruction\n   940→    void emitOperand(SpvWord word)\n   941→    {\n   942→        // Can only add operands if we are constructing an instruction (ie in _beginInst/_endInst)\n   943→        SLANG_ASSERT(m_currentInst || m_peekingOperands);\n   944→        m_operandStack.add(word);\n   945→    }\n   946→\n   947→    // The most common case of operand is an <id> that represents\n   948→    // some other instruction. In cases where we already have\n   949→    // an <id> we can emit it as a literal and the meaning is\n   950→    // the same. If we have a `SpvInst` we can look up or\n   951→    // generate an <id> for it.\n   952→\n   953→    /// Emit an operand to the current instruction, which references `src` by its <id>\n   954→    void emitOperand(SpvInst* src) { emitOperand(getID(src)); }\n   955→\n   956→    // Commonly, we will have an operand in the form of an `IRInst`\n   957→    // which might either represent an instruction we've already\n   958→    // emitted (e.g., because it came earlier in a function body)\n   959→    // or which we have yet to emit (because it is a global-scope\n   960→    // instruction  965→        SpvInst* spvSrc = ensureInst(src);\n   966→        emitOperand(getID(spvSrc));\n   967→    }\n   968→\n   969→    // Some instructions take a string as a literal operand,\n   970→    // which requires us to follow the SPIR-V rules to\n   971→    // encode the string into multiple operand words.\n   972→\n   973→    /// Emit an operand that is encoded as a literal string\n   974→    void emitOperand(UnownedStringSlice const& text)\n   975→    {\n   976→        // Can only emitOperands if we are in an instruction\n   977→        SLANG_ASSERT(m_currentInst || m_peekingOperands);\n encoded results.\n   982→        SLANG_ASSERT(text.indexOf(0) < 0);\n   983→        emitOperand(SpvLiteralBits::fromUnownedStringSlice(text));\n   984→    }\n   985→\n   986→    // Sometimes we will makeArray(SpvLiteralInteger::from32((int32_t)i)));\n  7105→            result =\n  7106→                emitOpStore(parent, (i == inst->getElementCount() - 1 ? inst : nullptr), addr, val);\n  7107→        }\n  7108→        return result;\n  7109→    }\n  7110→\n  7111→    SpvInst* emitSwizzleSet(SpvInstParent* parent, IRSwizzleSet* inst)\n  7112→    {\n  7113→        if (inst->getElementCount() == 1)\n  7114→        {\n  7115→            auto index = inst->getElementIndex(0);\n  7116→            if (auto intLit = as<IRIntLit>(index))\n  7117→                return emitOpCompositeInsert(\n  7118→                    parent,\n  7119→                    inst,\n  7120→                    inst->getFullType(),\n  7121→                    inst->getSource(),\n  7122→                    inst->getBase(),\n  7123→                    makeArray(SpvLiteralInteger::from32((uint32_t)intLit->value.intVal)));\n  7124→        }\n  7125→        auto resultVectorType = as<IRVectorType>(inst->getDataType());\n  7126→        List<SpvLiteralInteger> shuffleIndices;\n  7127→        shuffleIndices.setCount((Index)getIntVal(resultVectorType->getElementCount()));\n  7128→        for (Index i = 0; i < shuffleIndices.getCount(); i++)\n  7129→            shuffleIndices[i] = SpvLiteralInteger::from32((int32_t)i);\n  7130→\n  7131→        for (UInt i = 0; i < inst->getElementCount(); i++)\n  7132→        {\n  7133→            auto destIndex = (int32_t)getIntVal(inst->getElementIndex(i));\n  7134→            SLANG_ASSERT(destIndex <   auto source = inst->getSource();\n  7139→        if (!as<IRVectorType>(source->getDataType()))\n  7140→        {\n  7141→            IRBuilder builder(inst);\n  7142→            builder.setInsertBefore(inst);\n  7143→            source = builder.emitMakeVectorFromScalar(resultVectorType, source);\n  7144→        }\n  7145→        return emitOpVectorShuffle(\n  7146→            parent,\n  7147→            inst,\n  7148→            inst->getFullType(),\n  7149→            inst->getBase(),\n  7150→            inst->getSource(),\n  7151→            shuffleIndices.getArrayView());\n  7152→    }\n  7153→\n  7154→    IRPtrTypeBase* getPtrTypeWithAddressSpace(\n  7155→        IRPtrTypeBase* ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace,\n  7156→        AddressSpace addressSpace)\n  7157→    {\n  7158→        // If it's already ok, return as is\n  7159→        if (ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace->getAddressSpace() == addressSpace)\n  7160→            return ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace;\n  7161→\n  7162→        // It has an address space, but it doesn't match then fail, this\n  7163→        // indicates a problem with whatever's creating these types\n  7164→        SLANG_ASSERT(!ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace->hasAddressSpace());\n  7165→\n  7166→        IRBuilder builder(ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace);\n  7167→        return builder.getPtrType(\n  7168→            ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace->getOp(),\n  7169→            ptrTypeWithNoAddressSpace->getValueType(),\n  7170→            addressSpace);\n  7171→    }\n  7172→\n  7173→    SpvInst* emitStructuredBufferGetElementPtr(SpvInstParent* parent, IRInst* inst)\n  7174→    {\n  7175→        //\"%addr = OpAccessChain resultType*StorageBuffer resultId _0 const(int, 0) _1;\"\n  7176→        IRBuilder builder(inst);\n  7177→        auto addressSpace =\n  7178→            isSpirv14OrLater() ? AddressSpace::StorageBuffer : AddressSpace::Uniform;\n  7179→        return emitOpAccessChain(\n  7180→            parent,\n  7181→            inst,\n  7182→            // Make sure the resulting pointer has the correct storage class\n  7183→            getPtrTypeWithAddressSpace(cast<IRPtrTypeBase>(inst->getDataType()), addressSpace),\n  7184→            inst->getOperand(0),\n  7185→            makeArray(emitIntConstant(0, builder.getIntType()), ensureInst(inst->getOperand(1))));\n  7186→    }\n  7187→\n  7188→    SpvInst* emitStructuredBufferGetDimensions(SpvInstParent* parent, IRInst* inst)\n  7189→    {\n  7190→        IRBuilder builder(inst);\n  7191→        auto arrayLength = emitInst(\n  7192→            parent,\n  7193→            nullptr,\n  7194→            SpvOpArrayLength,\n  7195→            builder.getUIntType(),\n  7196→            kResultID,\n  7197→            inst->getOperand(0),\n  7198→            SpvLiteralInteger::from32(0));\n  7199→\n  7200→        // The buffer is a global parameter, so it's a pointer\n  7201→        auto bufPtrType = cast<IRPtrTypeBase>(inst->getOperand(0)->getDataType());\n  7202→        // It's lowered to a struct type..\n  7203→        auto bufType = cast<IRStructType>(bufPtrType->getValueType());\n  7204→        // containing an unsized array, specifically one with an explicit\n  7205→        // stride, which is not expressible in spirv_asm blocks\n  7206→        auto arrayType = cast<IRArrayTypeBase>(bufType->getFields().getFirst()->getFieldType());\n  7207→\n  7208→        // The element type should have a `SizeAndAlignment` decoration created during lowering.\n  7209→        auto sizeDecor =\n  7210→            arrayType->getElementType()->findDecoration<IRSizeAndAlignmentDecoration>();\n  7211→        SLANG_ASSERT(sizeDecor);\n  7212→        const auto stride = align(sizeDecor->getSize(), (int)sizeDecor->getAlignment());\n  7213→\n  7214→        auto strideOperand = emitIntConstant(stride, builder.getUIntType());\n  7215→        auto result =\n  7216→            emitOpCompositeConstruct(parent, inst, inst->getDataType(), arrayLength, strideOperand);\n  7217→        return result;\n  7218→    }\n  7219→\n  7220→    SpvInst* emitGetBufferPtr(SpvInstParent* parent, IRInst* inst)\n  7221→    {\n  7222→        IRBuilder builder(inst);\n  7223→        auto addressSpace =\n  7224→            isSpirv14OrLater() ? AddressSpace::StorageBuffer : AddressSpace::Uniform;\n  7225→        // The buffer is a global parameter, so it's a pointer\n  7226→        IRPtrTypeBase* bufPtrType = cast<IRPtrTypeBase>(inst->getOperand(0)->getDataType());\n  7227→        // It's lowered to a struct type..\n  7228→        IRStructType* bufType = cast<IRStructType>(bufPtrType->getValueType());\n  7229→        // containing an unsized array, specifically one with an explicit\n  7230→        // stride, which is not expressible in spirv_asm blocks\n  7231→        IRArrayTypeBase* arrayType =\n  7232→            cast<IRArrayTypeBase>(bufType->getFields().getFirst()->getFieldType());\n  7233→        return emitOpAccessChain(\n  7234→            parent,\n  7235→            inst,\n  7236→            builder.getPtrType(arrayType, addressSpace),\n  7237→            inst->getOperand(0),\n  7238→            makeArray(emitIntConstant(0, builder.getIntType())));\n  7239→    }\n  7240→\n  7241→    SpvInst* emitSwizzle(SpvInstParent* parent, IRSwizzle* inst)\n  7242→    {\n  7243→        if (inst->getElementCount() == 1)\n  7244→        {\n  7245→            const auto index = as<IRIntLit>(inst->getElementIndex(0))->getValue();\n  7246→            return emitOpCompositeExtract(\n  7247→                parent,\n  7248→                inst,\n  7249→
